event: Portrait of Saint Thomas the Apostle
symbols: spear or lance — Thomas's traditional attribute, the instrument of his martyrdom in India
thin golden halo — sanctity achieved through faith that overcame doubt
profile turned away — the apostle who initially turned from belief but was brought back by encounter with the risen Christ
open hand — the hand that would touch Christ's wounds and proclaim 'My Lord and my God'
description: A half-length portrait of a young dark-haired man shown in three-quarter profile, looking to the right. He wears a brown tunic over a white draped robe. In his right hand he holds a tall wooden staff or spear vertically. A thin golden halo circles his head. His left hand is extended with palm open. The background is dark gray with a diagonal line of light dividing the space. The figure is lit from the left, illuminating his face and the white drapery.
theological_description: Thomas, known as Didymus ('the Twin'), is forever associated with doubt and its resolution. When told of the Resurrection, he declared: 'Unless I see the nail marks in his hands and put my finger where the nails were, and put my hand into his side, I will not believe' (John 20:25). A week later, Jesus appeared and invited Thomas to touch his wounds. Thomas's response — 'My Lord and my God!' (John 20:28) — is the highest christological confession in the Fourth Gospel, explicitly declaring Jesus's divinity. Christ's response establishes the paradigm for all subsequent Christian faith: 'Because you have seen me, you have believed; blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' (John 20:29). Tradition holds that Thomas carried the Gospel to India, where he was martyred by a spear — the attribute he holds here. His journey from skepticism to the boldest confession of faith makes him the patron of all who struggle to believe.

scripture_refs.reason: Thomas's doubt and confession — 'My Lord and my God' — the defining episode of his life
Thomas saying 'Let us also go, that we may die with him' — his earlier courage
Thomas asking 'Lord, we don't know where you are going' — his honest questioning
